summary:
The Balkan Wars of 1912–1913 were two successive conflicts that dramatically reshaped the political map of southeastern Europe and contributed directly to the conditions leading to [[event:110|World War I]]. The First Balkan War (October 1912 – May 1913) united Bulgaria, Serbia, Greece, and Montenegro — the Balkan League — against the Ottoman Empire, which had ruled much of the peninsula for centuries. The allied states rapidly overran most of the empire's remaining European territories, including Macedonia, Thrace, and Albania.

The Second Balkan War (June–August 1913) erupted almost immediately when Bulgaria, dissatisfied with the territorial division, attacked its former allies Serbia and Greece. Romania and the Ottoman Empire joined the assault on Bulgaria, which suffered a swift and comprehensive defeat. The Treaty of Bucharest redrew borders again, leaving Bulgaria diminished and aggrieved. The wars effectively ended Ottoman rule in Europe outside a small zone around Istanbul, but the settlements left a volatile legacy: Serbia emerged greatly enlarged and ambitious, Austria-Hungary felt threatened by Serbian power, and the assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand in Sarajevo the following year ignited the larger European conflagration.

The Balkan Wars also foreshadowed later twentieth-century patterns of ethnic nationalism, population displacement, and atrocity. Hundreds of thousands of civilians were displaced, and numerous massacres of civilian populations were documented by international commissions, including one established by the Carnegie Endowment for International Peace.
